content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json).
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

  <typename>org.dxos.type.product-search.Provider</typename>
</object>

Let me analyze the page source I received from AutoTrader UK. Here's what I can determine:

**URL Structure** (from the skip-to-content link):
```
https://www.autotrader.co.uk/car-search?channel=cars&postcode=ws149el&make=Porsche&model=911&homeDeliveryAdverts=include&advertising-location=at_cars&year-to=2026
```

**Query parameters observed:**
- `channel` = cars (fixed)
- `postcode` = user location
- `make` = car make (e.g., Porsche)


- `model` = car model (e.g., 911)
- `homeDeliveryAdverts` = include (fixed)
- `advertising-location` = at_cars (fixed)
- `year-to` = max year
- `page` = page number (seen in detail links)
- `sort` = relevance (seen in detail links)

The available filter pills include make, model, max year manufactured, price, mileage, gearbox, body type, fuel type, engine size, and distance—all of which map to corresponding URL parameters I can use to construct search queries.

I'm also seeing additional parameters for price ranges, year ranges, maximum mileage, transmission type, body type, fuel type, and engine size bounds. For the listing structure, each car is wrapped in an `<li>` element with a test ID following the `id-{advertId}` pattern, and I can extract the title from a link within each listing.

Now I'm examining the price elements more closely — they don't have a data-testid attribute, so I'm looking at the generated class names instead. The listings are contained in a `ul` with class `sc-mddoqs-0`, and each `<li>` has class `sc-mddoqs-1`. I need to find a more stable selector for the price since the class names are dynamically generated. I'm noticing the images are lazy-loaded with placeholder divs, so I won't be able to extract them from the HTML directly. For the search schema, I can identify the key filter parameters from the URL structure: make, model, postcode, year range, price range, and a few other fields that are being cut off. the price span seems to be nested within a div, but all the class names appear to be dynamically generated, so I need to find a more reliable selector. Looking at the HTML structure, the price is consistently a span element within a specific div hierarchy, though without a data-testid attribute to anchor on. Let me look for a structural pattern that will be stable across different listings. I'm noticing the price element appears within the title link's hidden span alongside the car details, so I could extract it from there instead of relying on unstable CSS classes. Since the styled-component classes are generated and likely to change, I'll use the structural relationship with the title link as a more reliable fallback approach. I'm settling on using data-testid attributes where available—title, url, and subtitle all have clear test IDs—but for price I'll need to rely on the generated class names since there's no dedicated selector for it. I'm noticing the price span consistently has the class `sc-1mc7cl3-14`, so even though it's a generated class name, it should be stable enough to use as a selector for now. Writing the search schema... Writing request mapping schema... I'm noticing that most fields have convenient data-testid attributes, but price is trickier since the span containing the price value doesn't have one—I'll need to rely on its position in the DOM structure or the currency symbol pattern to extract it reliably. The hidden span inside the title link contains both the subtitle and price, but extracting the price would require regex parsing. I think the class-based selector approach is more practical—using `span.sc-1mc7cl3-14` to target the price span directly. However, the images are loaded lazily with just placeholder divs, so I won't be able to extract image URLs from the HTML alone since the actual images are rendered via JavaScript.

Now I'm trying to find a more reliable selector for the price field since the current class-based approach isn't stable—the generated class names keep changing, so I'm exploring whether there's a better way to target the price value, possibly through a data attribute or a more semantic element structure. I'm realizing the second div within that section contains the price information, with the actual price value nested inside a span with that generated class. Using the class selector is probably the most reliable approach here, even if it's not semantic — it's consistent across all the listings on the page.

searchSchema.description: The typed search fields as a JSON Schema (draft 2020-12) object, serialized to a JSON string.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You build search templates for vendor websites (retail, cars, real estate, etc.).
  A template has three parts that you persist together: a typed search schema, an HTTP
  request mapping, and a result mapping.
  
  Workflow:
  1. Call analyzeProvider with the Provider's ref to fetch the vendor site's page source.
  2. Inspect the source to understand how the site exposes search and lists results.
  3. Derive the search schema as a JSONSchema object whose properties are the typed search
     fields the user can fill in (string / number / boolean). Express ranges (e.g. min/max
     price, year) as separate number properties annotated accordingly.
  4. Derive the request mapping: prefer a formal API endpoint if one is discoverable;
     otherwise use the site's search URL with query parameters. Bind each request parameter
     to a search-schema field (use the 'min' / 'max' transform hints for range fields).
  5. Derive the result mapping: choose responseType ('html' or 'json'), an itemLocator that
     selects each listing, and per-field extractors for title, url, price, and image
     (CSS selector + attribute for html, JSONPath for json).
  6. Call setProviderTemplate with the Provider's ref, the request and result mappings, and
     the searchSchema as a JSON string (a JSON Schema object serialized with JSON.stringify,
     e.g. '{"type":"object","properties":{"make":{"type":"string","title":"Make"}}}').
  
  Be conservative: only include fields and selectors you can justify from the page source.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>echo://BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U/01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNT89</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.product-search.Provider</typename>
</object>
Analyze the AutoTrader provider in your context and generate and persist its search template (search schema, request mapping, result mapping) by calling analyzeProvider then setProviderTemplate.

- `make` - e.g., "Porsche"
- `model` - e.g., "911"


- `postcode` - e.g., "ws149el"
- `year-from` / `year-to` - year range
- `price-from` / `price-to` - price range
- `maximum-mileage` - mileage filter
- `transmission` - gearbox type
- `body-type` - vehicle body style
- `fuel-type` - fuel option
- `minimum-badge-engine-size` / `maximum-badge-engine-size` - engine size range
- `radius` - search distance from postcode
- `page` - pagination parameter

The API also requires fixed parameters for the channel and advertising location.
